relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Keilah's food and workers are attacked at the threshing floors.
David seeks direction toward rescue rather than toward reputation.
His followers name fear without being expelled from the mission.
He asks again, allowing discernment to hold their fear.
The city is relieved, though rescue still arrives through battle and seized life.
Abiathar carries priestly memory and an instrument of inquiry into exile.
Saul mistakes David's temporary enclosure for Divine approval of capture.
He mobilizes a nation against a city sheltering fugitives.
David recognizes the harm forming around Keilah.
He names the danger not only to himself but to the city.
The first answer confirms that Saul intends to come.
The second reveals what pressured local leaders are likely to do.
David leaves rather than force the city to absorb siege for him.
Life becomes movement among strongholds while pursuit continues.
David sees clearly that the king seeks his life.
Jonathan crosses the danger to strengthen David's hand.
He imagines a future beyond his own inheritance and names what his father knows.
Their covenant is renewed without possession of one another.
Local people carry information upward to the king.
They offer delivery of the fugitive in exchange for royal favor or safety.
Saul blesses surveillance as compassion toward himself.
He demands more precise tracking of the hunted man's movements.
Every refuge is mapped for centralized pursuit.
The search moves ahead of Saul into the wilderness.
Rock and desert become another temporary boundary.
Two groups circle one mountain while capture closes in.
Another crisis interrupts the king's private obsession.
Saul turns back, and the place receives a name of separation and escape.
David moves again to En-gedi, because rescue has not yet become safety.

contemplative_note: Discernment in this chapter is not private certainty detached from consequences. David asks again after his followers voice fear; he considers what siege would do to Keilah; he leaves rather than make a host city pay for his presence. Saul also uses Divine language, proving that religious confidence can accompany persecution. The contemplative test is what our certainty does to vulnerable people.
